--- SOURCE 1: What was a gap is now a chasm: Remote schooling, the digital ... ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/

SUMMARY:
COVID-19 resulted in the unexpected transition to remote learning for K-12 schools, exacerbating the existing digital divide and impacting the educational outcomes of marginalized youth. This article provides a review of recent literature on the educational impacts of COVID-19 in K-12 students with attention to the digital divide (i.e., unequal access to technology and the internet) and subsequent inequities in remote learning. The abrupt change to remote learning and the exacerbated digital divide during the COVID-19 pandemic had widespread educational impacts; however, research is still emerging on the educational impacts of COVID-19 not only in the U.S., but in K-12 students across the world.

What was a gap is now a chasm: Remote schooling, the digital divide, and educational inequities resulting from the COVID-19 pandemic
====================================================================================================================================

PMCID: PMC10259090 PMID: [37437380](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37437380/)

Abstract
--------

COVID-19 resulted in the unexpected transition to remote learning for K-12 schools, exacerbating the existing digital divide and impacting the educational outcomes of marginalized youth. This article reviews literature on the impacts of the pandemic on the educational outcomes of marginalized youth due to remote learning and the digital divide. Here, we provide an overview of the pandemic and remote schooling from an intersectional lens, discuss the impacts of the digital divide on learning for students during the pandemic, then consider impacts on the delivery of special education supports. Additionally, we review literature on the widening achievement gap in relation to the COVID-19 pandemic. Future directions for research and practice are discussed.

**Keywords:** marginalized youth, achievement gap, systemic inequity, intersectional identities

* * *

The COVID-19 pandemic brought an immeasurable amount of distress and hardship to the individuals and families across the globe whose lives were altered instantaneously. Schools were not immune to the changes as statewide mandatory lockdowns resulted in the abrupt transition from in-person learning to completely remote learning. In the age of technology, this adjustment, on the surface, seemed reasonable; however, the forced transition to remote learning amplified existing racial, socioeconomic, and educational disparities. The digital divide embodied existing inequitable access to resources that then had critical implications for students’ educational outcomes during the pandemic. This article provides a review of recent literature on the educational impacts of COVID-19 in K-12 students with attention to the digital divide (i.e., unequal access to technology and the internet) and subsequent inequities in remote learning. Recommendations for future directions are also provided as they relate to research and practice, which continue to be impacted by the effects of the pandemic.

1.1. Intersectional Identities: Race, Class, and Remote Learning
----------------------------------------------------------------

The educational impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic due to remote learning and the digital divide are best conceptualized through an intersectional lens. Youth situated at the intersecting margins of race (i.e., racially minoritized) and class (i.e., working class/economically disadvantaged) in both rural and urban settings were significantly impacted by the transitions to remote learning during COVID-19. However, there are few researchers who take these intersecting identities into account when examining the educational impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. Research instead focuses primarily on economic disparities in relation to educational outcomes and COVID-19.

During the early part of the pandemic, access to economic resources inarguably influenced the entire schooling process for youth and their families. For example, parents from many upper middle-class households had careers that afforded them the opportunity to work remotely [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib1)], providing more opportunities for support and oversight for school-attending youth in the household. Contrastingly, many working-class parents who did not experience job loss continued to work outside of the home due [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib1), [2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib2)] to having jobs that identified them as “essential workers.” As such, adolescents in working-class families were not only required to attend to their own schooling with less structure and support but also to attend to younger siblings in the household [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib3)]. Parents from working class or low-income households who were able to stay home with their children still experienced a myriad of challenges with homeschooling. For example, parents of elementary aged children in Hoskins and colleagues [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib4)] study in London discussed difficulties with homeschooling including managing multiple children or responsibilities, unfamiliarity with topics, and lack of training in teaching. Additionally, relative to higher-income households, parents from low-income households experienced greater challenges with navigating resources for teaching their children [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib3), [5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib5)]. Lastly, while youth from middle class families may have had larger homes and/or more spaces within the home to learn without disruption, this was a challenge for youth from working class homes who may have had to share learning spaces with siblings who were also learning remotely [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib6)].

It is important to note that class and race are not mutually exclusive (i.e., racial-wealth gap) given the history of economic oppression among people of color. Recent reports indicate that the racial-wealth gap between White and racially minoritized groups have remained steady in the U.S., with Black and Latino families most impacted [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib7)]. Accordingly, Black and Latino communities endured some of the most detrimental impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic [[[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib8), [[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib9), [[10]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib10)]. Relatedly, Black and Latino youth undoubtedly experienced the most drastic educational repercussions of the pandemic. In addition to the impacts of the racial-wealth gap on remote learning during the COVID-19 pandemic, the digital divide also served as a remote learning obstacle for many racially and socioeconomically disadvantaged youth [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib11)].

2.1. The Digital Divide and Intersectional Identities
-----------------------------------------------------

Unequal access to technology was not unique to the COVID-19 pandemic. This unequal access has been referred to as the “digital divide,” which disproportionately affects individuals from multiple intersecting identities and has persisted since the internet was created. One estimate suggested that 92% of all U.S. households had at least one computer, and 85% had an internet subscription in the year 2018 [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib12)]. However, computer ownership and internet subscriptions were lowest among households who identified as Black and households where at least one member had a disability. Additionally, the digital divide does not only encompass access to computers and internet subscriptions, but also quality and source of access are also important. For example, prior to the pandemic, youth from low-income households were less likely to have internet access beyond their cellular devices, access to good quality internet in their areas [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib13)], and may have had limited access to devices due to sharing with others in the household [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib4), [14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib14)]. Altogether, the digital divide pre-pandemic symbolized disparities in access to online extracurricular resources that could supplement students’ in-person learning; however, the COVID-19 pandemic shifted online learning from an auxiliary tool to the primary format for schooling, thus exacerbating these disparities.

Significant delays were noted in school districts’ abilities to provide technology to youth from marginalized backgrounds at the start of the pandemic [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib15)]. Indeed, one study highlighted that only two-thirds of youth in U.S. households with incomes less than $25,000 had computer and internet access for their children to engage in remote schooling [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib14)]. The lack of support for transitioning youth to remote learning resulted in the complete cancellation of schooling for some low-income youth and a mixture of paper packets and remote schooling for others [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib16), [17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib17)], which introduced complexities in where youth should focus their efforts and how assignments should be completed and submitted. For some youth and school systems, challenges with obtaining technology for remote schooling persisted into the 2020-2021 school year due to supply shortages and politics [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib18)].

Unsurprisingly, inequities in access to computers and the internet prior to the pandemic extended the digital divide to disparate use of technology and the development of technological skills and literacy among students [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib18), [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib19)], which likely impacted students’ ability to engage in school-based online learning platforms, dividing time between learning the platform and learning the content being taught. Students with access to computers and the internet in their homes are more likely to use computers and the internet [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib20)] and more likely to develop digital skills [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib21)] that would allow them to quickly transition to remote learning and access learning platforms with ease. For example, teachers have described negative impacts to student engagement due to the digital divide during the COVID-19 pandemic, noting the desire to connect with students online to discuss late assignments but being unable to due to students’ limited internet access [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib22)]. Educators have also struggled to form relational bonds with students impacted by the digital divide, which may negatively impact their educational perseverance [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib22)]. In short, students from these disadvantaged backgrounds, in addition to having limited and constrained access to instruction, may have also had to spend their time acquiring digital skills versus engaging in their schoolwork.

3.1. The Digital Divide and Special Education
---------------------------------------------

The digital divide also impacted the education of students with disabilities during the pandemic and remote learning. During the height of the pandemic in 2020, an estimated 15% of students were identified as having a disability [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib23)]. The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA, 2004) necessitates a legal mandate for K-12 schools to implement Individualized Education Plans (IEPs), which provide specially designed formal supports for students. As part of their IEPs, many students with disabilities receive special education services which relied on specialized interventions, accommodations, and other supports to meet their goals. A lack of clear policies regarding the implementation of IEPs in virtual settings [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib24)] and the abrupt shift to remote learning during the COVID-19 pandemic presented unique challenges for educators supporting students through service delivery [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib25), [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib26)].

Recent reports suggest that students who qualified for special education services under different categories were differentially affected by barriers to service provision through IEPs. For example, students with emotional and/or behavioral disorders comprise a particularly vulnerable group, as they are significantly more likely to be suspended, expelled, and arrested compared to their peers with IEPs for other disabilities and students in general education [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib27)]. One study found that beyond check-ins about social, emotional, and behavioral well-being, common emotional and behavioral strategies and interventions were not provided during the pandemic [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib28)]. Abrupt stops to services could have harmed students by interrupting the learning of coping skills and access to a safe, supportive environment for sharing their thoughts and feelings. Indeed, school personnel are essential to students with disabilities academic and personal development.

Finally, students who have related services on their IEPs (including various therapies) and students who are deaf/hard of hearing or blind/with visual impairments often require additional supports from service providers who may be itinerant or work part-time in the school system. In addition to the burden on the service providers to quickly shift their services to be delivered in a virtual format, students struggled to access these services due to inaccessible or non-existent technology (e.g., technology for teaching youth to read Braille) [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib29)]. A lack of access to these supports may have likely hindered students’ ability to keep up with instruction, thus resulting in detrimental impacts to youths’ educational outcomes.

4.1. Educational Impacts of COVID-19 and Remote Learning
--------------------------------------------------------

The abrupt change to remote learning and the exacerbated digital divide during the COVID-19 pandemic had widespread educational impacts; however, research is still emerging on the educational impacts of COVID-19 not only in the U.S., but in K-12 students across the world. Understanding the educational impacts of COVID-19 on youth requires researchers to not only consider academic achievement, but also academic-related experiences and behaviors (e.g., school engagement, motivation, etc.) that indirectly impact achievement. The amount of time that students spent learning virtually has been negatively associated with academic gains [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib36), [37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib37)]. This is interesting given that youth who attended school completely remote attended on average more days of school, suggesting that greater virtual attendance did not translate to more academic gains [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib36)]. However, these findings are also unsurprising given that the in-person school environment provided an opportunity for many students to focus better on learning than what could be afforded in the remote-learning environment [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib38)]. These findings are particularly important as racially marginalized students were more likely to continue learning virtually for longer periods of time compared to White students [[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib39)].

A 2020 projection of the pandemic's impact on academic impacts suggested that U.S. students would only make between 63 to 68% of the learning gains in reading and between 37 to 50% of the learning gains in mathematics relative to a typical school year [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib30)]. This projection has been corroborated by emerging studies, which demonstrate that academic gains following the start of the COVID-19 pandemic were significantly lower than seen in the years prior [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib31), [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib32)], with greater deficits noted in math, followed by reading [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib33)]. Studies examining learning loss in primary school students noted that learning loss increasingly worsened by grade, with fourth and fifth grade students experiencing the most learning loss. Comparatively, a meta-analysis looking at studies from around the world [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib34)] highlighted that older students (i.e., high school) fared better than younger students, suggesting that advanced elementary may have been a particularly sensitive developmental period for learning loss during the COVID-19 pandemic. It is important to note that youth who experienced educational challenges during remote learning, continued to experience challenges when returning to in-person schooling [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib35)], suggesting that the educational consequences of the pandemic may persist beyond the return to in-person schooling.

An amalgamation of factors interacted to create significant barriers that have impacted and will continue to impact the educational outcomes of K-12 youth. For instance, the transition to remote learning created significant barriers and stressors for teachers that undoubtedly shaped students’ academic outcomes. While many teachers already had access to technology, they were not always trained on how to engage their learners virtually or given adequate pedagogical support and resources for teaching online [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib38),[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib40),[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib41)]. Some teachers also perceived little support and engagement from parents [[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib42)], which was important given teachers’ limited access to support students more immediately. Additionally, while trying to navigate the digital environment for themselves, some teachers spent significant amounts of time troubleshooting technology issues with their students [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib38)]. Given the technology issues experienced by teachers and students, as well as the unknown regarding appropriate workloads for students during this transition, some teachers reported limiting the amount of new content they introduced to students [[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib43)] and ensuring mastery of previously introduced materials [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib38)]. These factors resulted in an increased workload for teachers [[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib44)] and increased stress [[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib41),[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib45)], which is associated with teacher burnout [[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib46)] and subsequently, turnover [[47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib47)]. Overall, teachers grappling with the realities of virtual teaching endured barriers and stress that inevitably impacted their abilities to positively impact the educational achievement of students during COVID-19, comparable to prior years, which will be seen in the achievement of students in the years to come.

Students also had unique experiences and perspectives associated with the transition to remote learning that are consequential for their educational outcomes. In addition to some students experiencing the previously mentioned technology issues, students reported that communication with teachers was a significant barrier [[48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib48)], which suggests that students’ support for navigating the digital learning environment likely varied greatly from teacher to teacher. Increased amounts of schoolwork were also a challenge for students [[49](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib49)], contrary to reports from teachers that they sought to give less work. With the challenges that students were facing, they also felt less motivation in school [[49](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib49), [50](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib50)]. While studies assessing students’ perspectives on learning during the pandemic are few, these studies demonstrate the unique barriers that may influence the academic outcomes of youth.

5.1. Widening of the Achievement Gap
------------------------------------

Persistent racial, ethnic, and economic disparities in academic achievement among students in the U.S. reflect longstanding systemic inequities that maintain unequal access to academic resources and opportunities [[51](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib51), [52](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib52)], but the magnitude of the COVID-19 pandemic’s impact on widening the achievement gap is unknown. Given the disparities noted due to the digital divide and the transition to remote learning, is it unsurprising that the achievement gap has widened due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Early in the pandemic, preliminary research using qualitative analyses and small sample sizes prompted concern for how the pandemic might disproportionately affect academic achievement among minoritized students [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib18), [53](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib53), [54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib54)]. Emerging evidence from larger datasets suggests that the pandemic was associated with widespread learning loss, revealing unprecedented declines in both math and reading achievement [[55](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib55), [56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib56)], including among minoritized students. Specifically, the pandemic resulted in greater score decreases in standardized tests among lower-performing students, students of color, and students from lower-income households [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib57), [58](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib58)]. Furthermore, data from Spring 2019 to Spring 2022 revealed that Hispanic/Latino, Black, and American Indian/Alaska Native students evinced larger declines in reading and math achievement compared to their White and Asian American peers [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib57)]. Data suggest that students are slowly recovering from pandemic-related learning loss, and it is estimated that students will require between one and more than five years to close the achievement gap [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib57), [59](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib59)]. Because students in high-poverty schools experienced larger pandemic-related achievement declines–and likely have less access to high quality academic resources–these students are facing a wider achievement gap and will likely require more time to recover [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259090/#bib57)]. The full effects of rapid changes to educational instruction may not be ascertained for years to come. As such, it will be important for researchers to examine educational impacts within the context of the digital divide.

6.1. Future Directions

--- SOURCE 2: Consequences of the Digital Divide in Education ---
URL: https://ctu.ieee.org/blog/2023/01/30/consequences-of-the-digital-divide-in-education/

SUMMARY:
**What Is the Digital Divide in Education?** The digital divide in education often comes down to available resources for students and teachers. The first hurdle for students and teachers to cross in the digital divide is internet access. How Does the Digital Divide Affect Education in the US? The digital divide in education has a lasting impact on students’ lives. K–12 students are not the only educational demographic affected by the digital divide. The IEEE Communications Society is also increasing awareness of the digital divide in education and other fields. To bridge the digital divide in education, all students must have access to the same basic tools, starting with broadband internet.

FULL CONTENT:
Published Time: 2023-01-30T17:58:35+00:00

Consequences of the Digital Divide in Education
===============================================

The digital divide is the socioeconomic gap created between communities, cultures, or individuals with different levels of access to digital tools. Those with unfettered access to broadband internet and computers have advantages over those who lack adequate internet connectivity, which can lead to far-reaching negative consequences for the latter.

The digital divide between countries can be even greater. In the US many regions takeaccess to the internet, computers, and smartphones for granted. In countries with underdeveloped supporting infrastructure, communities lack access to these tools.

Closing the gap of the digital divide is a daunting mission on a global scale. It requires advancements in digital infrastructure like internet backbone and globally available [wireless access](https://futurenetworks.ieee.org/tech-focus/may-2018/light-communications-for-wireless-local-area-networking), as well as equitable digital access for students across communities worldwide. The digital divide in education is growing as a result of the COVID-19 outbreak, and the subsequent restrictions have significant effects.

**What Is the Digital Divide in Education?**

The digital divide in education often comes down to available resources for students and teachers. This is usually directly related to what a family or school district can afford. The first hurdle for students and teachers to cross in the digital divide is internet access.

In most urban areas of the US, internet access is the simple part. Broadband internet became widely available in the early 2000s, and most regions have several internet providers. Services are not free, however, and access is limited by availability in rural areas.

**What Does the Digital Divide Look Like in Education?**

The digital divide is a growing concern in education. Broadband internet access is at the heart of the problem, and the reliance on distance learning during the COVID-19 pandemic has further complicated the issue.

Many schools turned to distance learning without adequate technological support. Students without internet access at home lost most, if not all, of their educational resources. Those who had previously relied on internet access at school, libraries, or neighbor’s houses lost that option under social distancing guidelines.

Students whose families can afford internet access and home computers are well equipped to deal with today’s challenges. Those without such necessities are on the other side of the gap. Providing resources to students in need is vital in shrinking the digital divide.

**What Problems Does the Digital Divide Cause in Education?**

The digital divide creates significant challenges for students. The problems caused by the digital divide in education includes both short-term and long-term challenges.

In the short term, students without broadband access may face learning challenges and limited access to relevant materials. This means they often struggle to complete homework, fall behind, receive lower grades, and sometimes even fail to graduate. Using smartphones to complete assignments causes additional problems since outdated learning software often functions poorly on smaller screens.

The long-term consequences of letting the divide grow affects students for years. Many middle skill jobs require digital skills that students on the wrong side of the digital divide never learn. This limits their career opportunities throughout their life. Additionally, less education generally leads to fewer job opportunities and less financial stability.

**Where Is the Digital Divide Most Prevalent in Education?**

The digital divide in education mirrors the socioeconomic gap, affecting rural communities, low-income families and other marginalized communities the most. The digital divide also disproportionately affects Native American, Black, and Latinx students.

Rural communities face educational disadvantages due to the limited availability of broadband internet access. Schools in small and rural communities often lack funding to provide technology to students for use in distance learning.

The gap widens for families with multiple school age children. These families face the added challenge of supplying multiple devices for multiple children to use simultaneously.

How Does the Digital Divide Affect Education in the US?
-------------------------------------------------------

From how teachers form lesson plans to how students learn and turn in homework, technology plays a significant role in education in the US. The COVID-19 pandemic has only expanded its use. During the pandemic, nearly all learning shifted to the digital space, effectively halting education for those students disadvantaged by the digital divide.

### Where Is the Digital Divide Visible in Education in the US?

Both sides of the digital divide are evident in education. Lacking technological resources results in lower academic performances while being on the other side of the digital divide produces a competitive advantage.

Low-income families are being left behind by the digital divide in education. Students have less access to the information they need to further their studies. Time wasted by inefficient technology also results in less time to learn.

Students with ample access to necessary digital technology have an academic advantage. From receiving high marks, to graduating and gaining entrance into a university, these students face fewer hurdles at every step of their education in the digital age.

### What Socioeconomic Issues Relate to the Educational Digital Divide in the US?

With the world in the middle of the [fourth industrial revolution](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/abstract/document/8573590), the gap between the rich and the poor is widening quickly. Internet access at home has gone from a luxury to a necessity. Consequently, the digital divide is also growing at an alarming rate.

The digital divide often correlates with lack of wealth. Low-income households might have to choose between buying food or paying for internet access to support their children’s education. Families that can afford reliable internet access and computers for their children are the least affected by the emergence of distance learning.

Another socioeconomic issue tied to the digital divide in education is funding. While schools may receive technology to combat the digital divide, they also require funding to implement the new technologies into curriculum and need to hire new staff to do so properly. Without financial support, educational technology may not be deployed or used effectively.

### How Does the Digital Divide Impact Students in the US?

There are concrete differences between students with reliable internet access versus those who do not. These differences range from graduation rates to lifetime financial earnings.

Students without reliable internet access are significantly less likely to graduate. A 2010 study published by Economic Inquiry concluded that teenagers with access to home computers were [6–8 percentage points](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1465-7295.2009.00218.x) more likely to graduate from high school than teenagers who did not. These statistics predate the COVID-19 pandemic. Students who are distance learning and have no access to a computer at home may now have no education at all.

The Federal Reserve shared that students with internet access at home out-earn those without at-home internet access by [$2 million](https://www.federalreserve.gov/pubs/ifdp/2008/958/ifdp958.pdf) over their lifetimes. The digital divide in education has a lasting impact on students’ lives.

The Digital Divide and the Adult Learner
----------------------------------------

K–12 students are not the only educational demographic affected by the digital divide. Colleges faced similar restrictions during to the COVID-19 pandemic, and many adult learners have limited access to necessary technology and less time to devote to their education.

### The Digital Divide in Higher Education

The digital divide affected higher education before the COVID-19 pandemic, but the rapid rise of remote learning is causing the gap to widen. Before the pandemic, many students lived on campus where they had access to reliable internet and computers. Commuter students also benefited from technology available on campus.

With the COVID-19 pandemic forcing many colleges to institute distance learning, students lost access to the vital resources they previously enjoyed on campus. Those without computers and internet at home may be left behind or forced to delay their education until their campus reopens.

### Impacts on Adult Learners

Adult learners face different challenges than child students when it comes to the digital divide. Some specific impacts for adult learners include financial stress, a lack of college resources, and a lack of networking opportunities.

Many adult learners are responsible for paying their own tuition, housing, and food. Add to this the expenses for a personal computer and broadband internet, and you have a recipe for significant financial distress. Adult learners often face similar financial challenges as parents of school age children.

The effects of distance learning are even greater for adult learners negatively impacted by the digital divide. They may become isolated as peer activities become more challenging without a communal college campus. Academic advisors, career counselors, and other support services have moved into the digital space, making these services difficult to access for those already struggling to secure broadband internet.

### Educational Obstacles for Adult Learners

Adult learners without adequate technology access, including easy broadband internet connection and computers, face significant education obstacles. They risk being left behind because of the digital divide.

Time is a vital resource for an adult learner. They must balance work, school, and daily responsibilities, often with no assistance. They might need to drive to the library for digital access, but in many cases the computers at the local library are slower and more outdated than those on a college campus.

Another major obstacle is digital literacy. Persistent and necessary use of a computer for education is relatively new. Adult learners who did not grow up with computers in school may lack the technology skills needed to use a computer the way higher education requires.

Bridging the Digital Divide in Education
----------------------------------------

The digital divide is still growing, but targeted steps are already being taken toward solutions. On a global scale and in small communities, individuals are acting to halt the growing digital divide.

### Partnerships to Close the Digital Divide

IEEE is uniting partners to address the digital divide. Government organizations, universities, and nonprofit organizations came together when IEEE hosted its Connecting the Unconnected Summit in November 2021. They aimed to discover solutions from the following perspectives:

*   Innovative technology applications to increase broadband access
*   Innovative business models resulting in increased affordability
*   Innovations to increase community enablement, or the likelihood that populations will choose to adopt available broadband access.

IEEE is [providing internet access](https://spectrum.ieee.org/providing-internet-to-rural-areas) in rural areas worldwide, exploring information and communication technologies (ICT) implementation in rural communities and developing countries. The IEEE [Communications Society](https://www.comsoc.org/) is also increasing awareness of the digital divide in education and other fields.

### Obstacles to Bridging the Digital Divide

To bridge the digital divide in education, all students must have access to the same basic tools, starting with broadband internet. Several obstacles stand in the way of universal access to affordable broadband.

Many remote regions require expanded broadband infrastructure to provide school age children necessary internet access. High costs ultimately leave internet access out of reach for local children.

In many cases, local governments could help close the digital divide among student populations. However, this requires local politicians to be educated on the digital divide, which is an additional hurdle to cross.

### School District Efforts to Bridge the Digital Divide

School districts and communities are looking inward for [solutions](https://www.yesmagazine.org/opinion/2020/09/09/school-covid-new-york) to the digital divide. School districts are providing underserved students access to necessary educational tools. Community-driven support programs include youth internships and mentoring programs.

While each educational community needs a unique approach for tackling the digital divide, solutions from one community can help inform others. School districts can play a pivotal role in providing resources for their students. Open communication between school districts can extend the reach of successful programs.

The Digital Divide in Education: Where We Stand
-----------------------------------------------

Amid the COVID-19 pandemic and the rapid rise in distance education, the digital divide in education is expanding rapidly and becoming increasingly problematic. Resource allocation challenges have made it difficult to achieve equitable education.

While eliminating the digital divide in education is not easy, individuals, communities, and organizations—like IEEE—are taking necessary steps toward closing the gap. Work is being done to provide stable broadband to rural areas and enable the education of poorer communities. Combating the digital divide is a priority to ensure that education continues despite the COVID-19 pandemic.

# How Broadband Impacts Remote Learning: Transforming Education for Good

Welcome to the era where remote learning is not just a trend, but a vital component of education. As technology continues to evolve, it offers incredible opportunities but also presents challenges for those without reliable internet access. At Fastwyre Broadband, we understand the crucial role broadband plays in education, and we’re dedicated to transforming remote learning through dependable internet solutions. 

**The Digital Divide in Education** 

Imagine a world where every student, regardless of their location or economic status, has seamless access to online education. Unfortunately, the reality is that not all students currently enjoy this privilege. This divide isn’t merely about technology; it’s an educational gap that must be addressed to ensure equal opportunities for learning. 

**How COVID-19 Highlighted the Importance of Broadband Access** 

The pandemic underscored the essential nature of broadband for education. As schools transitioned to online platforms, having reliable internet at home became a necessity for students to access resources, complete assignments, and participate in virtual classes. This shift highlighted the disparities in access and emphasized the need for comprehensive solutions. 

At Fastwyre Broadband, we are stepping up to ensure that every student can continue their education without interruption. Our focus is providing communities with the connectivity they need to thrive in the digital age. Our affordable broadband options provide options for families who need reliable internet. 

**The Impact of Broadband on Educational Attainment** 

Broadband access is directly linked to academic performance and future opportunities. Students with reliable internet can engage more effectively in online learning, enhancing their educational experience and preparing them for future success. Without it, students face significant barriers that can impact their grades and overall potential. 

Fastwyre Broadband is dedicated to leveling the playing field, providing students with the fast and reliable internet they need to reach their full potential. 

**Remote Learning and Broadband Access** 

The transition to online learning has transformed education, making reliable internet access a necessity. From participating in lessons to completing homework, connectivity is crucial. At Fastwyre Broadband, we are committed to ensuring that no student in our market is hindered by a lack of internet access. 

**The Challenges of Remote Instruction Without Broadband Access** 

Without reliable internet, students face numerous challenges in engaging with their education. Fastwyre Broadband is focused on eliminating these barriers by expanding our services and ensuring that every student in our market has the tools they need to succeed. 

**Addressing the Digital Divide in Education** 

Efforts to bridge the digital divide are more important than ever. Fastwyre Broadband is actively involved in expanding access to underserved communities, ensuring that every student in our market has the opportunity to succeed academically. We believe in the power of collaboration and innovation to bring about meaningful change. 

**The Importance of Devices and Internet Access** 

In today’s educational landscape, having both internet access and digital devices is crucial for student success. Fastwyre Broadband is committed to supporting initiatives that equip students with the necessary tools for effective learning, ensuring that connectivity is no longer a barrier. 

**Federal and State Initiatives** 

We recognize the importance of federal and state efforts to address tech equity challenges. Fastwyre Broadband supports these initiatives, contributing to a collective effort to provide reliable internet access to all students. 

**Conclusion** 

The role of broadband in remote learning is undeniable. Fastwyre Broadband is proud to be part of the movement to bridge the digital divide and ensure every student has access to quality education. By working together, we can create a world where every student is equipped for success in the digital age. 

Join us in our commitment to transforming education and providing equitable access to digital tools for all students. Together, we can make a lasting impact and pave the way for a brighter future.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Many students lack sufficient internet access, which hinders their ability to participate in remote learning, complete assignments, and access essential educational resources.
Limited access to computers and devices exacerbates educational inequities, making it challenging for students to engage effectively in online learning and complete their coursework.
In developing nations, inadequate infrastructure leads to significant digital divides, limiting educational opportunities and widening the gap between privileged and marginalized communities.
